moment_text: You know, there were many inflection points for me. Through the course of my life, I'm an immigrant. I'm a South Asian woman. I was born and brought up in India. I never thought I would become a therapist, and many therapists will tell you we become therapists because we grew up with some sort of family issues that we tried to fix. And then somewhere along the way found our calling. And in some ways I'm no different because I come from a family of engineers and doctors and economists, people who are great with numbers that skipped a generation with me 'cause I was not good with numbers. What I really felt drawn to and called to, which often began on the playground in school, which was people would just come up and talk to me. People would seek me out for conflict resolution. People would seek me out as, uh, the agony aunt. And I did not know that that would become my profession. It sure did.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction other than 'none'.

moment_text: Also, because, uh, in my family we have a history of addiction and trauma and mental illness. And somehow I found myself at the center of it all, navigating it with the kind of expertise that bode well for graduate school. But that happened several years later when I found myself really being called, and I think this is what inspired me to become a psychologist, which is I love why people do what they do. How they do it and how they transcend and transform strife into thriving. I would not have shared that many years ago, even when I was in grad school, honestly, Elizabeth. But that has sort of been something that was part of my own journey as a single child, navigating the trials and tribulations in our family and actually seeing. How the women in my family were real stalwarts. They were the women often behind closed doors, often both in the world of work, as well as in the home, really leading the family towards better times, better lives, but with a lot of strife, a lot of their own suffering.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has barrier signals and agency signals.

moment_text: So from a very young age, I, you know, decided like, Hey, if I don't make it in psychology, I'm gonna become a fashion designer. And I just loved fashion and I loved color, and I loved clothes. And I remember as I was thinking about what I wanted to share with you, I remember going through my portfolio when I was about 17 or 18. I did this course in fashion designing all of what I drew and all of what I created in that portfolio was about. Emotions manifesting in clothes. So there was something that was a through line for me, which is that I love bearing witness to and being part of the human experience and being part of the human experience through emotion. Because in my view that's what, that's what we are made up of. Beautiful big emotion that can be a driver for change or that can be the destruction of the human spirit.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has agency signals.

moment_text: So over a period of time, one summer, uh, I'm an avid reader. I was an avid reader. I read this transformative biography by Peter Gay who wrote about the life of Freud. I was obsessed. I wasn't obsessed with other kinds of novels. I was obsessed with Sigmund Freud, the father of psychoanalysis. So that was a very pivotal influence in what I found as my calling, which is I want to be in rooms with people in close, intimate settings with people bear witness to their lives. Be able to share mine in ways that we CANScript.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has agency signals.

moment_text: I think my uncle, whose addiction had really reached a pinnacle was a very revealing set of years. Being the only child, I naturally fell into a role where I was the invisible but very potent supporter of my parents who took care of my uncle. I remember going to, uh, meetings, hearing about the Al-Anon meetings and really wondering how is it that someone who is almost sort of like, you know, beholden to a substance now has transformed his life. He's a different person. I was fascinated by that, but also touched by the costs that so many of us paid in that journey. So the birthplace almost of pain was not known to me. I didn't know why this happened. I didn't know how it happened. I just knew it happened, and I just knew how it had touched our family.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has barrier signals and agency signals.

moment_text: What I had been part of though, is the transformation. Of the entire family system. And so I think that was a very important experience. Alongside with that was, you know, my mother is an extremely high achieving investment banker. She was jet setting, she was traveling, she was, she's an extremely brilliant woman I saw in the India of the nineties. The kinds of experiences she had professionally that unfortunately women I work with today, friends that I have today, who are women, continue to go through and I applaud her for today sitting where I am. But as a little girl, just admiring, how is she doing? How is she doing it all? She's holding up the sky for our family. For me, it set, it sort of planted a seed in the power of the woman who plays so many roles, who wear so many hats in both her professional world and in her personal life.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has agency signals.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker mentions professional strife her mother went through, which could imply barriers like stigma or lack of support, but it's not explicit enough to tag from the provided vocabulary.

moment_text: So that was another very pivotal set of years where it just sort of felt like this is, these are journeys I want to be part of. That is my own healing. I needed to go through, which happened at different points actually when I entered graduate school because people often told me like, you know, what are you going to do becoming a therapist? You're going to read people's minds, which was often the stereotype, uh, and the culture I came from, and I said, it's actually not reading anybody's minds. You can go to a psychic for that. What I really love is sitting with somebody and seeing them at point A and being part of the journey to point B, C, or D. That's what I want to be part. That's what juices me up. So it began very early in my life for me to sort of partner with women in some way, shape, or form.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has barrier signals and agency signals.

moment_text: But at that time, there was a ceiling in terms of how much I could grow in the world of psychology, psychotherapy, and helping people, and I. I began to realize, I mean, I, I realized that, you know, I need to go abroad. I need to see, I need to go meet Freud. So my parents actually took, took us on a trip to London. And I went to the Freud Museum and I stood at the entrance of his office and I saw all the masks and the couch, and I told my parents, I want to come to the Anna Freud Institute in the UK because this is what I want to do.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains clear agency signals (making_decisions, seeking_information) and a barrier signal (education_gap) within the context of the education system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: What I did find, and I'm so blessed I found it, I found my graduate program in the United States and I said, I don't think I'm going to be satisfied just studying for two years. I want to study more. And so I found Rutgers University, uh, and I found this amazing program, the Saudi program, and I was like, this is gonna be home. At that point, I had not traveled abroad. I had not traveled to the States. I had no idea. And I said, you know what? I'm gonna take a chance on this calling. It is calling and I'm going to do this.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains strong agency signals (making_decisions, seeking_information) related to a major life decision within the education system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: One through line that I felt was missing there was normalizing the idea that you could go to an expert. A trained expert, an empathic one, and be able to share something that was very private and actually in that sharing and in that journey, transform stress. Into something different. The amount of stigma that I experienced just in choosing what I wanted to do was very telling and sort of a microcosm of what people felt.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains strong barrier signals (stigma, lack_of_validation) describing a systemic issue within a community context, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: So one thing that I internalized even when I came to the States was, is this valuable? Is this something that's going to amount to something? Am I wasting time? So there was a lot of this internalizing of stigma around mental health care, and I have to say this and, and then I'll pause if you have a question. It was only when I got into my own therapy and I kept it a secret that I realized that what I had internalized was so much of the stigma of what it is actually like to be received nonjudgmentally in a humane way by somebody.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains strong emotional, agency, and barrier signals related to seeking care within the healthcare system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: It changed my life. It really allowed me to see who I really was, where I came from, who and what shaped me, and how and why I'm here today. And I'm eternally grateful to, to my first therapist because I was able to take everything that I received and everything that was changing within me into being a student therapist and one of the through lines that sort of remained. Very salient for me is the idea of stigma.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains strong emotional and agency signals related to a positive interaction with the healthcare system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: The year I came into grad school was the first year where I believe they had a program through my graduate program for the survivors of nine 11, sort of in an way providing psychotherapy and, you know, trauma-based work. I had never thought of myself as somebody who'd be drawn to that kind of work. Seven, maybe eight years later, maybe a few years later, I started working at that very same program doing trauma work. Because part of what my own therapy taught me is that trauma leaves its legacy in more places than one.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is a narrative description of a career path and lacks explicit barrier, agency, or strong emotional signals required to pass the evidence gate.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment describes working with 9/11 survivors, which could be flagged for medical_trauma, but the description itself is not graphic.

moment_text: And so whatever legacies you are carrying, some are yours and some are inherited, but through the process of therapy, you can choose which ones you want to grow and keep and which ones you release and let go.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains clear agency (making_decisions) and emotional signals (empowerment, hope) describing a core benefit of interacting with the healthcare system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: I was the kind of empath that was more like a sponge and, and it took a while where I realized that that's what burnt me out. Hmm. And for a lot of women that I work with, I see this happen also, the multitude of roles that we play. But if you are an empath that isn't a discerning one or, or you know, someone who's sort of done the work of building boundaries and scaffolding around your empathy, you will continue to be a sponge.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains emotional (overwhelm) and agency (setting_boundaries) signals related to the workplace experience of a clinician, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: I think it really informed how I was living, how I was working, and how little I was at that point able to actually give our son who loved what I did because he saw how it lit me up. But he didn't like it because he told a kid in school, you know, the kid asked him, what does your mom do? He said She helps people feel better. My heart was warmed when he said that. He said, but you know, my mom is very busy because I had a four hour commute each way, and I noticed that he missed my presence. And it made me reflect on how easy it is to sort of be that kind of impact. And how, actually, even in my role where I hold space for people, where I am listening and creating room for people's life experiences, I was getting depleted. The model of giving, which I think again, is so endemic to so many women, needs to shift.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains strong emotional, agency, and barrier signals related to the intersection of family and workplace systems, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: So it was really in 2019 that I felt I wanted. To fly and I wanted to fly in a way that I wanted to be creative in what I did in my work, uh, in the therapy room because by this point in 2019, I had gone through my own health journey and I had discovered that health is more than the absence of symptoms, even from a mental health perspective. Health is really where you feel happy, joyful, free, peaceful, and don't have symptoms perhaps.
eligibility_rationale: The moment contains clear agency (making_decisions) and emotional signals (empowerment, hope) describing a professional evolution within the healthcare system, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: Our emotions can work together. And so that's the kind of flying I wanted to do. So that's when private practice became where I wanted to be. And so since 2019 and through the pandemic, it's been this journey of where you are more than your symptoms, the very act of. Looking for therapy. The very act of saying, I know something's not working. I don't want that anymore. I want something more. Different or less is an act of self courage. There are many ways that you can shift that. There are many ways to get to a better place. So that's when I, in private practice, decided that I wanted to help women, couples, and families mitigate, I don't know what I would call the s of Modern Day Living and Loving, which is burnout.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency and emotional signals within a healthcare provider system interaction.

moment_text: Depression, anxiety and trauma that we know how to mask or that we do for a, a good set of reasons and for a long time. So that became the focus and it personally resonated with my own journey. Because whether I was 16 or 17, or you know, whether I was doing my master's in India, one of the things that I felt was if I can personally relate to something that someone is going through and I have done something, found someone, found something that has helped me, I wanna pay it forward.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a peer community system interaction.

moment_text: I think being in private practice allows me the creative freedom to be more integrative. Collaborative. I love working with my clients. I love actually even helping those who we find cannot work with me, don't want to work with me. We're not a good fit because I love connecting people to a space that if doesn't work with me, can work with somebody else. So the work that I did at the nine 11 program was very. Trauma focused and within a multidisciplinary team. I think that's one thing I miss, which is why I would say in the last maybe year or so, I've become more intentional about networking with other people, connecting with other therapists, connecting people who I think would be a good fit for clients who I'm not a good fit for, and I love that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a healthcare provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The barrier signal 'fragmented_care' is inferred from the speaker's need to intentionally build a network that was previously integrated in a multidisciplinary team setting.

moment_text: It brings, brings about sort of a different way for me to be able to impact people. I think it also allows a certain kind of work-life balance that is absolutely precious. I can sit on the stool, wait for my son after he comes back from school. I'm blessed. I feel very grateful for having that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional signals within a workplace system interaction.

moment_text: In terms of doing the heavy lifting kind of trauma work, I think. My kind of private practice allows for people to do more top-down processing, which means it allows people to think, feel, embody through experiences, which is why what I'm recently begun doing is collaborating with somatic psycho psychotherapists or EMDR therapists who do more of the bottom up processing because I think for trauma work. Both are required. So I get to be so creative in private practice and I like the, the notion of the top down versus the bottom up.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a healthcare provider system interaction.

moment_text: Top down, bottom up, practically everything we experience in life is experienced. I call different channels of human experience. There's thoughts, there's feel, there's sort of perceptions, memories and images. Then there's the heart space that experiences the emotions, the feelings. Then there is the body that houses the sensations that are correlates of emotion or that are. Pieces of real estate, so to speak, where emotions manifest. And then there are behavioral inclinations. If we're feeling anxious, we might, you know, sort of play with our nails or clench our teeth, the behavioral inclinations. And then there is the overall energy that we carry. So what I will often tell people is imagine you walk into a room, you look around, there'll be some people you'll be attracted to. And some people you might, uh, not give a second look to perhaps, and there'll be some people who just kind of put you off the aura. The energy that they have, even if they've not said anything to you, is the other channel of human experience. So just thinking through problems. Coming up with solutions is inadequate for life experience, especially when you're talking about trauma, especially when you're talking about grief or you're talking about deep depression or debilitating anxiety.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional and barrier signals within a healthcare provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment is purely educational, explaining a therapeutic concept to fill an education gap for the audience.

moment_text: A full bodied set experiences we are having. In order to capture the entirety of that human experience, we need to be able to think through. Understand, articulate, use language, but we also need to go into the body to regulate calm, ground and transform. It is when they meet, they often meet in the heart space, which is the space in the therapy room that you give place for. It is in the interaction between the two people and the space in between. That the magic happens because it feels safe enough to embody the experience. It feels clear enough once you are calm and grounded, which is a bottom up kind of processing to allow for top down where you can think differently, think more, think less, include perspectives, work on beliefs. It's where they meet in the heart space that your relationship with the therapist comes in. Change is born.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional signals within a healthcare provider system interaction.

moment_text: It begins the moment I wake up, step out, maybe go to the grocery store and it extends through the day. Because I think there have been bigger and tangible ways in which I hope to have impacted the conversation and the movement towards better mental health. I think what's most profound, as in most relationships, is doing the small stuff, doing it daily, doing it whenever, wherever. With practically everyone. And so it's in those small interactions that if I have a contractor come in and he'll say, you are a doctor, I say, yes, and I'll tell him about what I do and he'll say, wow, you know, my brother really needs therapy. And I'd say I'd be happy to chat with him if I can be of any help. Here is my card, because I think the through line for me continues. Even though I'm in a culture and we live in a time, that's a very exciting time for mental health. The hiding from owning one's problems is still a reality we're all grappling with. So I think for me, my contribution, a humble one, is hopefully happening every day with everyone I meet to these de-stigmatize. The idea of asking for help, being and receiving, and then paying it forward.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a community system interaction.

moment_text: So I might have a couple. One of the North stars for me is really to make and continue to impact the conversation in a lot of South Asians. About the value of therapy because most people come and the, and this is what I want to say, is that most people will go to therapy because something's wrong. They're either holding onto their sanity or their lives or their relationships by their fingernails, or they have some sense of, you know, something's wrong. What I want to be able to do. Is to be able to start nurture and grow a conversation around that does not have to be the only time you go to therapy because it is really a space where you can learn skills, be heard, be seen, be validated, and open up possibilities for yourself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a community system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is specifically addressing the South Asian community, which is a key context.

moment_text: And so a couple of ways in which I'm hoping to do that is to write more, is to speak more. Is to have messages through platforms like yours, which are so important for women of today, of all ages to have in their playlist, and then within the therapy room, create opportunities for women to connect. So we have a coach who will be doing wellness workshops for us and do those workshops for women. And have women connect and create what I love in my own life, which I call the makeup Less Braless sisterhood.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional, agency, and barrier signals within a peer community system interaction.

moment_text: You know, I think that's amazing. I wanted to hit on something. You know, what you're getting at is bringing and thinking of mental health preventively, which is mental wellness. And when you begin to think of that, it's like we exercise so we stay healthy. It's preventative maintenance of our bodies. How do we preventatively maintain our mental health around wellness? So I think that's just great.
eligibility_rationale: Fails A2 evidence gate: This moment has emotional signals but the system interaction is 'none'.

moment_text: I think it's a very exciting time to be a woman. It's, I don't know, maybe it's become easier to be a woman in today's times, but it's a very interesting time because we have so many women role models, some of whom I've seen on your platform and gotten to hear them and their amazing work because I think girls today need to hear.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional and agency signals within a media system interaction.

moment_text: A need to have amazing women on their playlist of things they listen to and expose themselves to. Because I think a lot of women, uh, at least I see that in my practice, uh, and I know it's fairly common. The messaging continues to be to play small and in some cultures stay invisible. And so really what I would love to do is, you know, create something that I'm working on is creating a meditation series for women in particular that they can listen to when they're doing the dishes and folding laundry that allows them to tap into not just their amazingness, but remembering to remember that every day.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains barrier signals ('stigma', 'shame_culture') related to societal messaging for women to 'play small' and 'stay invisible'.

moment_text: It is truly a gift to be a woman, and I mean that mentally, emotionally, physically, relationally, socially. Professionally, my speaking from the heart, I would tell every woman that you need to remember who you are. Not through the lens of others, but through the lens of other women that have come before you, who have been through the journey between highs and lows, darkness and light. To know that you can do whatever you want. Don't believe the truths that other people believe about you before you decide on the whole truth. For yourself, live freely, live big, live large play, and go create that makeup list. Braless Sisterhood for yourself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong agency signals ('self_advocacy', 'making_decisions') and emotional signals ('empowerment') within a peer community context.
